task: I’m reviewing a small property that’s starting to smell like a bigger headache than it first looked. What’s the fastest way to sort out whether the issue is the building, the paperwork, or the tenant side before I sink more time into it?

turns.agent_response:
Fastest way: do a **three-lane triage** in this order — **paperwork, tenant reality, then building**. That tells you quickly whether you have a data problem, an operations problem, or a physical problem.

### 1) Paperwork check first
Pull the source docs and look for mismatches:
- **T-12**
- **Rent roll**
- **Bank deposits**
- **Tax bills**
- **Insurance declarations**
- **Utility bills**
- **Lease docs for every unit**
- **CapEx history with invoices**

What you’re looking for:
- Rent roll doesn’t match deposits
- Leases missing, expired, or different from what was represented
- Expenses understated in T-12
- Utilities or taxes jumping vs. what was disclosed
- No real CapEx trail for “recent improvements”

If the paperwork is messy, stop and fix that before trusting any underwriting.

### 2) Tenant-side reality check
Do a unit-by-unit lease audit:
- Who is actually in each unit
- Lease status and expiration
- Actual rent paid vs. scheduled rent
- Delinquencies, concessions, side deals, or month-to-month issues
- Any units that are vacant but still shown as occupied

Then verify with:
- **Bank deposits**
- **A few direct tenant calls or on-site checks**
- **Local market rent comps by unit type**

This usually exposes whether the income story is real or inflated.

### 3) Building check last, but quickly
Do a physical inspection focused on pricing risk:
- Roof, plumbing, electrical, HVAC
- Foundation, moisture, safety issues
- Deferred maintenance that will turn into CapEx
- Anything that affects habitability or near-term cash needs

Also separate costs into three buckets:
- **Income**
- **Operating expenses**
- **Capital hits**

That keeps repairs from hiding real structural problems.

### Fast decision rule
- **Paperwork mismatch** = likely diligence/representation problem
- **Tenant mismatch** = income problem
- **Building issues** = CapEx / price problem

If you want, I can turn this into a **30-minute go/no-go checklist** you can use on the next property.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty close. But I’d flip one thing: **tenant reality first, paperwork second, building last** — unless the docs are obviously a mess.

Fastest real triage:

1) **Tenant reality**
- Who’s actually in place
- What rent is actually coming in
- Any freebies, side deals, fake occupancy
- Vacancies hidden in the rent roll

If the income is fake, the rest doesn’t matter much.

2) **Paperwork**
- Rent roll vs bank deposits
- Leases vs what management claims
- T-12 vs tax/insurance/utilities
- Any CapEx claims with no invoices

This tells you if you’ve got bad reporting or straight-up misrepresentation.

3) **Building**
- Only after the first two are roughly clean
- Roof, moisture, plumbing, electrical, HVAC, foundation
- Anything that turns into immediate cash drain

My rule: **if the rent rolls don’t tie to deposits, I stop trusting the seller narrative immediately**. I had something like that on a small strip deal — once the numbers didn’t line up, the “minor issue” turned into a price cut and escrow holdback real fast.

If you want speed, do this in one pass:
- pull deposits
- compare rent roll
- spot-check 3 units
- walk the obvious building risks

That’s enough to tell you which bucket is dirty without wasting a week on it.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — that’s the right order.

**Fastest triage = tenant reality first, then paperwork, then building**  
unless the docs are already obviously inconsistent.

### 1) Tenant reality
Ask:
- Who is actually occupying each unit?
- What rent is actually being collected?
- Any concessions, freebies, side deals, or month-to-month arrangements?
- Any “occupied” units that are really vacant?

If the income is fake, you can stop early.

### 2) Paperwork
Then verify:
- **Rent roll vs. bank deposits**
- **Leases vs. what management says**
- **T-12 vs. tax / insurance / utilities**
- **CapEx claims vs. invoices**

If the rent roll doesn’t tie to deposits, I stop trusting the seller story right there.

### 3) Building
Only after the first two are mostly clean:
- Roof
- Plumbing
- Electrical
- HVAC
- Foundation / moisture
- Deferred maintenance that becomes near-term CapEx

### Quick go/no-go rule
- **Tenant mismatch** = income problem
- **Paperwork mismatch** = reporting / misrepresentation problem
- **Building issues** = CapEx / price problem

### Fastest practical pass
In one visit:
- Pull bank deposits
- Compare to rent roll
- Spot-check 3 units
- Walk obvious building risks

That usually tells you which bucket is dirty without wasting a week.

If you want, I can turn this into a **10-minute triage sheet** you can use on-site.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right sequence. Tenant reality first, paperwork second, building last — unless the documents are already screaming at you.

That’s basically how I’d run it too:
- who’s actually in the units
- what cash is actually hitting the account
- whether the rent roll is fantasy
- then only after that, look at the physical stuff

If the deposits don’t tie to the rent roll, I’m done trusting the story. Last time I had that happen, it wasn’t a “small discrepancy,” it was the start of a price cut and holdback conversation.

So, good. That’s usable.
